name: Fraud / Abuse Review
notes: Threat-model assumptions that do NOT transfer: fraud pipelines operate under a known adversarial equilibrium where attackers actively probe detection thresholds using real transaction data; this means the background distribution is already partially shaped by adversarial behavior, which is atypical for SOC or content-moderation pipelines. Asymmetry attack surface: transaction-level noise can be generated programmatically at very low cost, enabling high-volume queue flooding without the textual creativity needed in content-moderation attacks; dedup based on transaction fingerprints is less effective because each transaction carries unique identifiers by design. Classifier_scale is relatively low here because risk scores are expected to span a wide range — this limits the attacker's ability to use small perturbations to push scores across thresholds. Relevant mitigations: velocity-based intake limiting per account and device to bound noise volume; entity-linkage graphs that flag when a new low-risk case shares identifiers with a known high-risk case; post-hoc reconciliation that re-scores dedup-suppressed inputs when a linked sentinel is confirmed. Transfer limits: the adversarially-shaped background distribution assumption does not transfer to SOC or trust-safety pipelines where background inputs are mostly benign; the moderate false-negative cost reflects financial reversibility and does not apply to life-safety or irreversible-action profiles.
background_templates: Risk note: transaction batch {batch} matched routine spending pattern for segment {segment}.
Case summary: device {device} produced low-confidence anomaly during {window}.
Review note: refund request from merchant class {merchant} aligned with expected seasonal behavior.
sentinel_inputs: High-risk takeover case: device fingerprint changed alongside password reset and payout reroute attempt on account cedar-55.
Fraud escalation: transaction narrative shows stacked anomaly flags, new payee burst, and geo-velocity mismatch.
Abuse alert: repeated refund exploitation pattern confirmed across linked accounts and payment instruments.
